items.passage: Comets are icy bodies that travel along long, looping orbits around the Sun. As they move closer, the heat from sunlight turns their frozen gases into glowing clouds. This glow is often shaped into a bright tail that may stretch across millions of miles. Although they look fiery, these objects formed in the cold outer regions of the early solar system. Their study tends to reveal useful clues about the materials that shaped distant planets.

items.passage: The periodic table arranges every known chemical element into a clear and orderly grid. Each row holds atoms that carry a similar weight yet differ in their behavior. Elements that fall in one column often act alike because they share the same outer electrons. This neat pattern was first noticed when the elements were sorted by their atomic mass. Although gaps once remained, the table is now used to predict how unknown substances might react.

items.passage: Wind turbines convert the moving energy of air into clean electrical power. Their long blades spin when the wind pushes against their gently curved faces. This spinning is used to drive a generator that sends out a steady flow of current. Engineers often place these tall machines on open hills or along windy coastal plains. Although the breeze may shift, modern designs adjust their angle to capture as much force as possible.

items.passage: Writing systems allow human speech to be recorded and shared across great spans of time. The first forms used simple signs that stood for whole objects or familiar ideas. Over time these marks were slowly reshaped into symbols that stand for single spoken sounds. Such alphabets made reading easier because people only needed to learn a small set of letters. Although they began as art, these scripts tend to spread knowledge among ordinary citizens.

items.passage: The number zero represents the complete absence of any quantity or measured amount. Ancient traders first relied on this mark to keep their account columns aligned. Later thinkers in India treated zero as a true number that could be freely added or removed. Although the idea seemed strange, it was applied to extend mathematics beyond the limits of plain counting. Today zero remains vital because it tends to anchor nearly every modern calculation.

items.passage: Hurricanes are powerful storms that gather their strength over warm tropical ocean waters. Rising moist air cools quickly and builds the thick clouds that drop heavy rain. The trapped heat feeds the storm and often causes its winds to spin much faster. Although the system looks violent, a calm center known as the eye appears at its core. Forecasters watch these storms closely because they may bring dangerous flooding to low coastal towns.
